dependencies.description: NETCONF requires SSHv2 (port 830). RESTCONF requires HTTPS (TLS on port 443). gNMI requires gRPC with TLS (port 50051). All three depend on a functioning PKI or pre-shared key infrastructure for secure transport.
dependencies.name: SSH/TLS

dependencies.description: All three interfaces require user authentication — local, TACACS+, or RADIUS. Authorization controls which YANG paths a user can read or modify. Without AAA, sessions are rejected.
dependencies.name: AAA (Authentication, Authorization, Accounting)

dependencies.description: Clients must use YANG models that match the device's advertised capabilities. Models are bundled with the device software and vary by OS version. OpenConfig models provide cross-vendor compatibility; native models provide full feature coverage.
dependencies.name: YANG models

failure_modes.description: Client cannot establish a NETCONF session. SSH connection to port 830 is refused, times out, or fails authentication. The device never sends the NETCONF hello message. This blocks all programmatic configuration management via NETCONF.

resolutions.action: Enable NETCONF: 'netconf-yang' (IOS-XE), 'feature netconf' (NX-OS), 'netconf-yang agent ssh' (IOS-XR)
Enable NETCONF: 'netconf-yang' (IOS-XE), 'feature netconf' (NX-OS), 'netconf-yang agent ssh' (IOS-XR)
Enable NETCONF: 'netconf-yang' (IOS-XE), 'feature netconf' (NX-OS), 'netconf-yang agent ssh' (IOS-XR)
Verify SSH is enabled and operational: 'show ip ssh' or 'show ssh server'
Verify SSH is enabled and operational: 'show ip ssh' or 'show ssh server'
Verify SSH is enabled and operational: 'show ip ssh' or 'show ssh server'
Check and adjust ACLs on management interface or VTY lines to permit TCP 830
Check and adjust ACLs on management interface or VTY lines to permit TCP 830
Check and adjust ACLs on management interface or VTY lines to permit TCP 830
Review 'show netconf-yang sessions' and close stale sessions if max is reached
Review 'show netconf-yang sessions' and close stale sessions if max is reached
Review 'show netconf-yang sessions' and close stale sessions if max is reached
Configure AAA: local user with privilege 15, or TACACS+/RADIUS method list
Configure AAA: local user with privilege 15, or TACACS+/RADIUS method list
Configure AAA: local user with privilege 15, or TACACS+/RADIUS method list
Ensure SSH key exchange algorithms match between client and server
Ensure SSH key exchange algorithms match between client and server
Ensure SSH key exchange algorithms match between client and server

root_causes.cause: NETCONF subsystem not enabled on the device (missing 'netconf-yang' on IOS-XE, 'feature netconf' on NX-OS)
SSH not configured or SSH server disabled
ACL or control-plane policing blocking TCP port 830
Maximum concurrent NETCONF sessions reached (platform limit)
AAA not configured — NETCONF requires valid authentication (local or TACACS+/RADIUS)
SSH version mismatch — client requires SSHv2 but device offers SSHv1 only
VRF mismatch — SSH listener not bound to the management VRF the client is reaching

failure_modes.scenario: NETCONF session not establishing — SSH to port 830 fails
symptoms: Client receives SSH connection refused or timeout on port 830
SSH connects but NETCONF subsystem is not available
'show netconf-yang sessions' shows no active sessions
Client log shows authentication failure or capability mismatch
Max-sessions-reached error in device logs

failure_modes.description: Client automation sends NETCONF/RESTCONF/gNMI operations using a YANG model version or structure that does not match what the device supports. The device returns rpc-error with 'unknown element', 'data-missing', or 'invalid-value'. This is the most common programmability issue — especially when mixing OpenConfig and native (Cisco) YANG models, or when the device software was upgraded and YANG models changed.

resolutions.action: Retrieve device capabilities first: check hello message or 'show netconf-yang capabilities' to see which models are supported
Retrieve device capabilities first: check hello message or 'show netconf-yang capabilities' to see which models are supported
Retrieve device capabilities first: check hello message or 'show netconf-yang capabilities' to see which models are supported
Use YANG Suite, pyang, or yangsuite to validate the model against the device's advertised schema
Use YANG Suite, pyang, or yangsuite to validate the model against the device's advertised schema
Use YANG Suite, pyang, or yangsuite to validate the model against the device's advertised schema
Match model to device: use Cisco-native models (Cisco-IOS-XE-*, Cisco-NX-OS-*) for full feature coverage, OpenConfig for multi-vendor
Match model to device: use Cisco-native models (Cisco-IOS-XE-*, Cisco-NX-OS-*) for full feature coverage, OpenConfig for multi-vendor
Match model to device: use Cisco-native models (Cisco-IOS-XE-*, Cisco-NX-OS-*) for full feature coverage, OpenConfig for multi-vendor
Pin automation to specific YANG model revisions and test after every software upgrade
Pin automation to specific YANG model revisions and test after every software upgrade
Pin automation to specific YANG model revisions and test after every software upgrade
Use NETCONF get-schema RPC to download the exact YANG module from the device
Use NETCONF get-schema RPC to download the exact YANG module from the device
Use NETCONF get-schema RPC to download the exact YANG module from the device

root_causes.cause: Client using OpenConfig model but device only supports Cisco-native model (or vice versa)
YANG model revision changed between software versions — leaf names, containers, or types differ
Incorrect YANG path — container/list/leaf hierarchy does not match device schema
Client not filtering capabilities — sending model elements the device did not advertise in hello
Unsupported leaf type or enumeration value in the specific software version

failure_modes.scenario: YANG model mismatch — client sends operations with wrong model
symptoms: rpc-error with error-tag 'unknown-element' or 'malformed-message'
RESTCONF returns 400 Bad Request or 404 Not Found on valid-looking paths
Partial configuration applied — some leaves accepted, others rejected
gNMI Get returns empty response for paths that should have data
Automation scripts fail after device software upgrade

verify_commands.look_for: Search for the YANG module name and revision date that the client is trying to use. If absent, the device does not support that model.

failure_modes.description: Client receives 401 Unauthorized or 403 Forbidden when attempting RESTCONF operations. The HTTPS connection itself may succeed (TLS handshake completes) but the application-layer authentication or authorization fails. This blocks all REST-based automation and often indicates missing HTTP server configuration or RBAC policy issues.

resolutions.action: Enable RESTCONF: 'restconf' (IOS-XE) + 'ip http secure-server' + 'ip http server'
Enable RESTCONF: 'restconf' (IOS-XE) + 'ip http secure-server' + 'ip http server'
Enable RESTCONF: 'restconf' (IOS-XE) + 'ip http secure-server' + 'ip http server'
Configure AAA for HTTP: 'ip http authentication local' or point to TACACS+/RADIUS
Configure AAA for HTTP: 'ip http authentication local' or point to TACACS+/RADIUS
Configure AAA for HTTP: 'ip http authentication local' or point to TACACS+/RADIUS
Create local user with privilege 15: 'username <user> privilege 15 secret <pass>'
Create local user with privilege 15: 'username <user> privilege 15 secret <pass>'
Create local user with privilege 15: 'username <user> privilege 15 secret <pass>'
Install a trusted certificate or configure client to skip TLS verification for lab/test
Install a trusted certificate or configure client to skip TLS verification for lab/test
Install a trusted certificate or configure client to skip TLS verification for lab/test
Check RBAC policies: ensure user's privilege level permits the requested YANG path operations
Check RBAC policies: ensure user's privilege level permits the requested YANG path operations
Check RBAC policies: ensure user's privilege level permits the requested YANG path operations

root_causes.cause: Wrong credentials or credentials not configured for RESTCONF access
HTTPS server not enabled — missing 'ip http secure-server' (IOS-XE)
RESTCONF feature not enabled — missing 'restconf' configuration command
Self-signed certificate not trusted by client — TLS verification fails
RBAC or privilege-level restrictions — user authenticated but lacks permission for the operation
'ip http server' not enabled — required on IOS-XE for RESTCONF redirect functionality
AAA method list not configured for HTTP — 'ip http authentication' missing

failure_modes.scenario: RESTCONF authentication failure — HTTPS connection rejected
symptoms: HTTP 401 Unauthorized on RESTCONF requests
HTTP 403 Forbidden — user authenticated but operation denied
HTTPS connection refused on port 443 (HTTP server not running)
TLS handshake failure — certificate not trusted by client
curl/Postman returns 'connection refused' to device RESTCONF URL

verify_commands.confirms_if: Both HTTP and HTTPS servers enabled confirms RESTCONF transport is ready
verify_commands.look_for: Check 'HTTP server status: Enabled', 'HTTP secure server status: Enabled', and the listening port (443). Also check authentication method in use.

failure_modes.description: A gNMI telemetry subscription that was previously streaming data stops delivering updates to the collector. The gRPC channel may remain open but no SubscribeResponse messages arrive, or the channel drops entirely. Monitoring dashboards go stale, alerting becomes blind, and network visibility is lost.

resolutions.action: Renew TLS certificates before expiry; set up certificate rotation monitoring
Renew TLS certificates before expiry; set up certificate rotation monitoring
Renew TLS certificates before expiry; set up certificate rotation monitoring
Check gNMI process health: 'show grpc status' and device syslogs for crashes
Check gNMI process health: 'show grpc status' and device syslogs for crashes
Check gNMI process health: 'show grpc status' and device syslogs for crashes
Reduce subscription scope: fewer paths, longer sample intervals, use on-change only where appropriate
Reduce subscription scope: fewer paths, longer sample intervals, use on-change only where appropriate
Reduce subscription scope: fewer paths, longer sample intervals, use on-change only where appropriate
Verify network path: ensure firewalls have long idle timeouts (3600s+) for gRPC streams
Verify network path: ensure firewalls have long idle timeouts (3600s+) for gRPC streams
Verify network path: ensure firewalls have long idle timeouts (3600s+) for gRPC streams
Implement client-side reconnect logic with exponential backoff
Implement client-side reconnect logic with exponential backoff
Implement client-side reconnect logic with exponential backoff
Monitor device CPU/memory — telemetry can be resource-intensive with many sensor paths
Monitor device CPU/memory — telemetry can be resource-intensive with many sensor paths
Monitor device CPU/memory — telemetry can be resource-intensive with many sensor paths

root_causes.cause: TLS certificate expired — gRPC channel drops on TLS renegotiation
gNMI server process crashed or was restarted (software bug, memory exhaustion)
Subscription path invalid or no longer available after software/config change
Too many subscriptions or sensor paths — device resource exhaustion (CPU, memory, buffer)
Network path issue — MTU problem or firewall state timeout on long-lived gRPC stream
Client not reading stream fast enough — server-side buffer overflow and stream reset

failure_modes.scenario: gNMI telemetry subscription dropping — streaming telemetry stops
symptoms: Telemetry collector shows data gap — metrics stop updating
gRPC stream returns error code UNAVAILABLE or CANCELLED
Device logs show gNMI/gRPC process crash or restart
'show telemetry ietf subscription' shows subscription in error state
Collector sees initial burst of data then silence

verify_commands.confirms_if: Subscription in non-Valid state confirms the subscription has failed
verify_commands.look_for: Check subscription state — 'Valid' means active, anything else indicates a problem. Look at sensor-path and update interval.

verify_commands.confirms_if: gRPC server not running or high error count confirms server-side issue
verify_commands.look_for: Check if gRPC server is running, listening port, TLS status, and number of active connections. Look for error counters.

verify_commands.confirms_if: gRPC server not running or high error count confirms server-side issue
verify_commands.look_for: Check if gRPC server is running, listening port, TLS status, and number of active connections. Look for error counters.

verify_commands.confirms_if: Excessive CPU on telemetry processes confirms resource exhaustion
verify_commands.look_for: Check CPU utilization of gNMI/gRPC/telemetry processes. High CPU may indicate too many subscriptions or a process loop.

failure_modes.description: A NETCONF edit-config, RESTCONF PUT/PATCH, or gNMI Set operation fails. The device returns rpc-error (NETCONF), HTTP 409/500 (RESTCONF), or gRPC ABORTED/FAILED_PRECONDITION (gNMI). The intended configuration is not applied, leaving the device in its previous state. In the worst case, a partial commit may leave the device in an inconsistent state.

resolutions.action: Check for active locks: 'show configuration sessions' (IOS-XR) or 'show netconf-yang sessions' and close stale sessions
Check for active locks: 'show configuration sessions' (IOS-XR) or 'show netconf-yang sessions' and close stale sessions
Check for active locks: 'show configuration sessions' (IOS-XR) or 'show netconf-yang sessions' and close stale sessions
Use discard-changes RPC to clean up a dirty candidate datastore before retrying
Use discard-changes RPC to clean up a dirty candidate datastore before retrying
Use discard-changes RPC to clean up a dirty candidate datastore before retrying
For confirmed-commit: send confirming commit within the timeout, or increase the confirm-timeout value
For confirmed-commit: send confirming commit within the timeout, or increase the confirm-timeout value
For confirmed-commit: send confirming commit within the timeout, or increase the confirm-timeout value
Validate config before committing: use test-option 'test-only' to check without applying
Validate config before committing: use test-option 'test-only' to check without applying
Validate config before committing: use test-option 'test-only' to check without applying
Ensure all referenced objects exist before pushing config (interfaces, VRFs, route-maps, ACLs)
Ensure all referenced objects exist before pushing config (interfaces, VRFs, route-maps, ACLs)
Ensure all referenced objects exist before pushing config (interfaces, VRFs, route-maps, ACLs)
Use rollback-on-error error-option to prevent partial commits
Use rollback-on-error error-option to prevent partial commits
Use rollback-on-error error-option to prevent partial commits

root_causes.cause: Config datastore locked by another NETCONF session — 'lock-denied' error
Validation error in candidate configuration — conflicting or incomplete config
Confirmed-commit timeout — commit was conditional and confirm was not sent in time
Resource unavailable — hardware table full, TCAM exhausted, license missing
Dependency error — config references an object that does not exist (e.g., interface, VRF, ACL)
Device in config-exclusive mode — another user/session has exclusive config access

failure_modes.scenario: Config replace/commit failure — edit-config fails to apply
symptoms: rpc-error with error-tag 'lock-denied', 'in-use', or 'resource-denied'
RESTCONF 409 Conflict — another session holds the config lock
edit-config succeeds but commit fails with validation error
Confirmed-commit timer expires and config is rolled back automatically
gNMI Set returns FAILED_PRECONDITION — config dependency not met

verify_commands.confirms_if: Active session with lock confirms lock contention as root cause
verify_commands.look_for: Shows active configuration sessions and who holds locks. Look for sessions in 'uncommitted' state or held locks. (IOS-XR primary command; IOS-XE shows config replace sessions.)

verify_commands.confirms_if: Active session with lock confirms lock contention as root cause
verify_commands.look_for: Shows active configuration sessions and who holds locks. Look for sessions in 'uncommitted' state or held locks. (IOS-XR primary command; IOS-XE shows config replace sessions.)

verify_commands.confirms_if: Active session with lock confirms lock contention as root cause
verify_commands.look_for: Shows active configuration sessions and who holds locks. Look for sessions in 'uncommitted' state or held locks. (IOS-XR primary command; IOS-XE shows config replace sessions.)

verify_commands.confirms_if: Stale session from disconnected client confirms lock-denied cause
verify_commands.look_for: List all active NETCONF sessions. Check session IDs, source IPs, and how long they have been connected. Stale sessions may be holding locks.

verify_commands.confirms_if: Stale session from disconnected client confirms lock-denied cause
verify_commands.look_for: List all active NETCONF sessions. Check session IDs, source IPs, and how long they have been connected. Stale sessions may be holding locks.

verify_commands.confirms_if: Stale session from disconnected client confirms lock-denied cause
verify_commands.look_for: List all active NETCONF sessions. Check session IDs, source IPs, and how long they have been connected. Stale sessions may be holding locks.

verify_commands.confirms_if: Specific validation error identifies the exact config element that failed
verify_commands.look_for: Shows the last failed configuration attempt with error details. Available on IOS-XR. Look for the specific validation error or dependency failure.

verify_commands.confirms_if: Specific validation error identifies the exact config element that failed
verify_commands.look_for: Shows the last failed configuration attempt with error details. Available on IOS-XR. Look for the specific validation error or dependency failure.

verify_commands.confirms_if: Specific validation error identifies the exact config element that failed
verify_commands.look_for: Shows the last failed configuration attempt with error details. Available on IOS-XR. Look for the specific validation error or dependency failure.

messages.description: NETCONF capability exchange — sent by both client and server immediately after SSH channel establishment. Advertises supported YANG models, NETCONF versions, and optional capabilities (candidate, confirmed-commit, xpath, etc.).
messages.name: hello
messages.when_sent: Immediately after SSH subsystem 'netconf' is activated

messages.description: NETCONF retrieval RPCs. 'get' retrieves operational state + config; 'get-config' retrieves only configuration from a specified datastore (running, candidate, startup). RESTCONF equivalents: GET on data resource. gNMI equivalent: Get RPC.
messages.name: get / get-config
messages.when_sent: Client requests operational or configuration data

messages.description: NETCONF configuration modification RPC. Applies changes to the target datastore with merge/replace/create/delete/remove operations. RESTCONF equivalents: PUT (replace), PATCH (merge), POST (create), DELETE. gNMI equivalent: Set RPC (update/replace/delete).
messages.name: edit-config
messages.when_sent: Client pushes configuration changes to the device

messages.description: NETCONF server response to any RPC. Contains either the requested data (for get/get-config), an <ok/> confirmation (for edit-config/commit), or <rpc-error> with error details. RESTCONF equivalents: HTTP response with status code + body. gNMI equivalent: GetResponse / SetResponse.
messages.name: rpc-reply
messages.when_sent: Server responds to a client RPC request

messages.description: Asynchronous event or telemetry push. NETCONF: <notification> with eventTime and payload. gNMI: SubscribeResponse with Update messages containing timestamped path-value pairs. Used for streaming telemetry (sample-based or on-change).
messages.name: notification / Subscribe-Update
messages.when_sent: Device pushes data at configured sample interval, on data change (on-change subscription), or on event occurrence

purpose: Model-driven programmatic configuration management, operational state retrieval, and streaming telemetry via YANG-modeled data over SSH (NETCONF), HTTPS (RESTCONF), or gRPC (gNMI)

states.description: No active NETCONF/RESTCONF/gNMI session. Transport is not established — no SSH channel (NETCONF), no HTTPS connection (RESTCONF), and no gRPC channel (gNMI) to the device.
states.name: DISCONNECTED

states.description: Transport established and NETCONF hello exchanged (capability negotiation complete), RESTCONF HTTPS session authenticated, or gNMI gRPC channel open. Device is ready to accept RPCs, REST operations, or gNMI requests. This is the normal operational state for request/response interactions.
states.name: SESSION_OPEN

states.description: A telemetry subscription is active — the device is streaming updates to the collector via NETCONF notifications or gNMI Subscribe RPCs. Data is pushed at configured intervals (sample/on-change). This is the normal state for telemetry.
states.name: SUBSCRIBED

timers.description: Maximum time a NETCONF session can remain idle (no RPC exchange) before the device closes it. Prevents session leaks from disconnected or abandoned clients.
timers.name: netconf-session-idle-timeout
timers.on_expiry: Device closes the NETCONF session and releases the session slot. If the client held a config lock, the lock is released.
timers.tuning_advice: Lower to 300-600s in environments with many automation clients to prevent session exhaustion. Set to 0 only for persistent monitoring sessions.

timers.description: Interval at which the gNMI server sends a heartbeat message on an active subscription to indicate the stream is still alive, even when no data changes have occurred.
timers.name: gnmi-subscription-heartbeat-interval
timers.on_expiry: Collector receives a heartbeat update confirming the subscription is still active. If heartbeats stop, the collector should assume the subscription has failed.
timers.tuning_advice: Set to 60-120s for on-change subscriptions so the collector can detect stream failures quickly without waiting for the next data change.

transitions.failure_if_stuck: Transport or authentication failure — SSH not enabled, certificates invalid, AAA misconfigured, ACL blocking port, or max sessions reached
transitions.trigger: SSH connection established on port 830 and NETCONF hello exchanged (capability advertisement); or HTTPS connection established and authenticated (RESTCONF); or gRPC channel opened with TLS handshake (gNMI)

transitions.failure_if_stuck: Subscription path invalid, YANG model not supported, or device resource limits exceeded (too many concurrent subscriptions)
transitions.trigger: Client issues a NETCONF create-subscription RPC, or gNMI Subscribe RPC with valid YANG path and encoding. Device begins streaming telemetry updates.
Client sends close-session RPC, idle timeout expires, TCP connection drops, SSH keepalive failure, or TLS session terminated

config_examples:
netconf-yang
restconf
ip http server
ip http secure-server
ip http authentication local
!
telemetry ietf subscription 100
 encoding encode-kvgpb
 filter xpath /process-cpu-ios-xe-oper:cpu-usage/cpu-utilization/five-seconds
 source-address 10.1.1.1
 stream yang-push
 update-policy periodic 1000
 receiver ip address 10.2.2.2 57000 protocol grpc-tcp

notes.note: 'netconf-yang' enables NETCONF. RESTCONF requires 'restconf' + 'ip http secure-server'. Model-driven telemetry configured via 'telemetry ietf subscription'. Strong OpenConfig support across all major feature areas. YANG Suite (free tool) recommended for model exploration and RPC testing. gNMI requires 'gnxi' or 'gnmi-yang' depending on version (17.6+).

config_examples:
netconf-yang agent ssh
ssh server v2
ssh server netconf vrf default
!
grpc
 port 57400
 no-tls
 address-family ipv4

notes.note: NETCONF enabled by default on most versions — 'netconf-yang agent ssh' to configure explicitly. gNMI via 'grpc' configuration stanza with TLS certificate. Strong YANG and OpenConfig support — both Cisco-native (Cisco-IOS-XR-*) and OpenConfig models available. 'show netconf-yang clients' shows active NETCONF sessions. Candidate datastore with explicit commit model (commit-confirm, commit-replace supported).

config_examples:
feature netconf
feature restconf
feature grpc
! NX-API (legacy REST):
feature nxapi
nxapi http port 80
nxapi https port 443

notes.note: 'feature netconf' enables NETCONF over SSH on port 830. 'feature restconf' enables RESTCONF. NX-API (REST) is the primary programmatic interface on NX-OS and predates RESTCONF support. DME (Data Management Engine) models are the native NX-OS YANG models. OpenConfig support is limited compared to IOS-XE and IOS-XR. For gNMI, use 'feature grpc' (10.2+).

config_examples:
# sonic-gnmi runs as a container, configured via:
# /etc/sonic/config_db.json -> GNMI section
# Or via CLI:
sudo config feature state gnmi enabled
# gNMI default port: 8080 (insecure) or 50051 (TLS)
# TLS certificates: /etc/sonic/telemetry/

notes.note: gNMI is the primary programmatic interface for SONiC — served by the sonic-gnmi container. OpenConfig models supported for interface, BGP, routing, and system configuration. RESTCONF is not a standard SONiC interface. CONFIG_DB (Redis) is the configuration source of truth; gNMI operations translate to CONFIG_DB writes via the management framework. 'config' CLI, gNMI Set, and direct CONFIG_DB writes all converge on the same datastore.
